content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 22, 2017

Bill Sator
City Attorney
City of Tracy
333 Civic Center Plaza
Tracy, CA 95376

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-253

Dear Mr. Sartor:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090 on behalf of Tracy City Councilmember Juana Dement. Please note that we are not providing advice under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Joaquin County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit Councilmember Dement from participating in decisions to amend, negotiate, or extend the current agreement with Skyview Aviation LLC (Skyview), a company that employs Councilmember Dement's spouse.

CONCLUSION

Yes. Under Section 1090, Councilmember Dement would have a financial interest in such agreement. However, the rule of necessity applies and the City and Skyview may enter into the agreement so long as Councilmember Dement does not make or participate in any decisions

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-253
Page No. 2

involving the agreement. Additionally, because the remedy in this situation under the Act would be
the same, we do not analyze the potential conflicts of interest under the Act.

FACTS

Councilmember Dement was appointed to the Tracy City Council on January 3, 2017.
Skyview has been serving as the City's Specialized Aeronautical Services Operator (SASO) at the
Tracy Municipal Airport since November 2007 following a Request for Proposals process. In
November 2007, the City entered into a five-year SASO Agreement with Skyview, in which
Skyview agreed to provide certain services such as repair and aircraft rental services and a flight
school at the Tracy Municipal Airport. Skyview was also authorized to provide other services such
as air charter, aircraft sales, and sport plane assembly services to customers. In exchange, Skyview
receives a discount in aviation fuel sales and pays a monthly rent payment to the City.

In 2012, the City and Skyview agreed to renew the SASO Agreement for another five years
and executed a new agreement. The 2012 SASO Agreement was amended in 2014 to allow
Skyview to install and use a temporary portable building at the airport to accommodate its expanded
flight school operations. The 2012 SASO Agreement was amended again in 2016 to allow Skyview
to operate fuel trucks for the flight school's use only.

On May 31, 2017, the City notified Skyview that Skyview was in default of the 2012 SASO
Agreement due to its failure to pay for fuel within ten days of receiving the fuel, as required under
Section 18.1 of the Agreement. The City informed Skyview that it had 30 days to cure the default
by paying the outstanding invoices in their entirety. On June 19, 2017, Skyview submitted a notice
of intent to renew the 2012 SASO Agreement which is set to expire on December 31, 2017, in
accordance with Section 7 of the Agreement, which provides, in part:

If CITY has not notified LESSEE of any breach to this AGREEMENT
which remains uncured as of the last day prior to the expiration of the
initial term or an extended term, as appropriate, and upon timely request
by LESSEE to CITY of its intent to extend the term prior to an option of
extension hereunder, CITY shall grant up to two five-year term
extension periods. The criteria to be used by the CITY in evaluating
satisfactory performance of this agreement shall include but not be
limited to LESSEE's performance of those sections of this
AGREEMENT dealing with rent payment, hazardous waste
management, LEASED FACILITY maintenance, insurance, compliance
with laws, and effectuation of LESSEE's general responsibilities....

LESSEE shall provide CITY with written notification of LESSEE's
interest in having LESSOR consider entering into any extension period
contemplated under this section at least six months prior to the lease
expiration date.

Skyview submitted a timely notice of intent to renew to the City; however, it remains in
default due to non-payment of outstanding fuel invoices. Skyview has asked the City to agree to a

File No. A-17-253
Page No. 3

payment plan for these outstanding invoices. Staff can accept partial payments for the outstanding
invoices; however, per the terms of the 2012 SASO Agreement, Skyview will remain in default
until all outstanding invoices are paid.

According to Councilmember Dement, her spouse has been employed by Skyview for nine
years as office staff and has no ownership interest in the business. He does not participate in
contract negotiations, including preparing or assisting in the preparation of requests for proposals
on behalf of Skyview. Skyview currently has nine employees. In addition to the agreement with the
City of Tracy, Skyview has an agreement with the Turlock Regional Aviation Association to serve
as the SASO for the Turlock Municipal Airport.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 provides, in pertinent part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county,
district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any
contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are
members." Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-
member bodies, whether elected or appointed, at both state and local level, including certain
independent contractors.

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest,"² and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might
profit from it in any way. Employees have been found to have a financial interest in a contract that
involves their employer, even where the contract would not result in a change in income or directly
involve the employee, because the employee has an overall interest in the financial success of the
firm and continued employment.³

Here, Councilmember Dement "stands in the shoes of her spouse" for purposes of Section
1090⁴ with respect to any contracts between the City and Skyview. Therefore, she has a prohibited
financial interest in future contracts between the City and Skyview, including one to modify,
extend, or renegotiate the existing SASO Agreement.

When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, as
here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain. Instead, the
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract.⁵ The Legislature has created
various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the financial interest involved is

² People v Homig (1996) 48 Cal App.4th 289, 333.

³ 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001)

⁴ Thorpe v. Long Beach Community College (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 655, 659; Nielsen v. Richards (1925) 75
Cal.App. 680; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 258 (2006).

⁵ Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 647- 649.

File No. A-17-253
Page No. 4

deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a "noninterest," as defined in Section
1091.5. However, none of these exceptions are applicable to the facts presented.6

Nonetheless, in limited circumstances, the "rule of necessity" allows a contract otherwise
violating Section 1090 to ensure that essential government functions are performed. (69
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 102, 109 (1986).) In this case, Skyview has been the City's Specialized
Aeronautical Services Operator for the previous ten years and Councilmember Dement's spouse has
been employed by Skyview for nine years as office staff with no ownership interest in the business.
Moreover, the nature of the services provided are essential to the operation of the City's airport and
of a specialized nature. To the extent that there are other alternative service providers, it appears the
number of alternatives would be very limited. Accordingly, we find that the rule of necessity
applies and permits the City to enter the agreement. Councilmember Dement, however, must
abstain from any participation in the decision.7

Government Code Section 87100 of the Act

Additionally, because Councilmember Dement must abstain from any participation in the
approval of contracts between the City and Skyview under Section 1090, we do not need to further
analyze the potential conflict of interest issue raised under the Act. Her abstention under Section
1090 would also satisfy the requirements of the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

BGL:jgl

6 Section 1091(b)(2), the most relevant exception to the facts provided, establishes a remote interest for
longstanding employees of a contracting party but does not apply when a company has less than 10 employees.

7 When the rule of necessity applies to a member of a multi-member board, the Attorney General's Office has
determined that the interested board member must abstain from any participation in the decision. (See 89
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 217 (2006).)

Yes. Under Section 1090, Councilmember Dement would have a financial interest in such 
agreement. However, the rule of necessity applies and the City and Skyview may enter into the 
agreement so long as Councilmember Dement does not make or participate in any decisions 
The regulations oflhe Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. involving the agreement. Additionally, because the remedy in this situation under the Act would be 
the same, we do not analyze the potential conflicts of interest under the Act.
